title: Overcoming Logistical Pressures In Russia India Crude Trade
section_path: Overcoming Logistical Pressures In Russia India Crude Trade
section_path_text: Overcoming Logistical Pressures In Russia India Crude Trade
summary:
Main
Russia-India crude flows can rebound to normal levels, contingent on additions to vessel supply of shadow fleet. By Delia He
India's crude imports from Russia fell 20% m-o-m in July. Sanctions enforcement has intensified in recent months - especially with mounting US scrutiny on India, EU sanctions on Vadinar Refinery, and the EU's dynamic price cap regulation on Russia to be implemented in Sep-25.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
Russia-India crude flows can rebound to normal levels, contingent on additions to vessel supply of shadow fleet. By Delia He
India's crude imports from Russia fell 20% m-o-m in July.

children.title: India is key demand outlet for Russian crude
section_path: India is key demand outlet for Russian crude
children.section_path_text: India is key demand outlet for Russian crude
children.summary: Russia crude grades have accounted for over one-third of India's total crude slate so far this year. On the supply side, Russia has remained heavily reliant on India as its largest demand outlet.

children.title: Logistical bottlenecks from insufficient non-sanctioned tonnage can be bypassed relatively quickly
section_path: Logistical bottlenecks from insufficient non-sanctioned tonnage can be bypassed relatively quickly
children.section_path_text: Logistical bottlenecks from insufficient non-sanctioned tonnage can be bypassed relatively quickly
children.summary: Vessels sanctioned by OFAC have been the most effective for neutralising the servicing of the Russia-to-India crude trade. As this trend is expected to persist, all newly OFAC-sanctioned vessels (both Russia-related and Iran-related designations) in 2025 would cease discharging at India's ports.

children.title: Additional Russia-trading tonnage could come from S&P market, dark fleet candidates
section_path: Additional Russia-trading tonnage could come from S&P market, dark fleet candidates
children.section_path_text: Additional Russia-trading tonnage could come from S&P market, dark fleet candidates
children.summary: The demand for additional tonnage required to maintain Russia-to-India crude flows would likely be met by ageing or operationally less competitive vessels finding renewed utility through the sale-and-purchase (S&P) market. Rising freight rates - as Argus reports, Urals freight from the Baltic- and Black Sea-to-India WC has surged by 11% and 17% w-o-w respectively - alongside the need to replace sanctioned and defecting vessels, would readily incentivise additional tonnage to join the Russia-trading fleet.
